title: A cell type-aware framework for nominating non-coding variants in Mendelian regulatory disorders

match.title: A cell type-aware framework for nominating non-coding variants in Mendelian regulatory disorders
match.authorString: Lee AS, Ayers LJ, Kosicki M, Chan W, Fozo LN, Pratt BM, Collins TE, Zhao B, Rose MF, Sanchis-Juan A, Fu JM, Wong I, Zhao X, Tenney AP, Lee C, Laricchia KM, Barry BJ, Bradford VR, Lek M, MacArthur DG, Lee EA, Talkowski ME, Brand H, Pennacchio LA, Engle EC.
match.abstractText:
ABSTRACT

Unsolved Mendelian cases often lack obvious pathogenic coding variants, suggesting potential non-coding etiologies. Here, we present a single cell multi-omic framework integrating embryonic mouse chromatin accessibility, histone modification, and gene expression assays to discover cranial motor neuron (cMN) cis -regulatory elements and subsequently nominate candidate non-coding variants in the congenital cranial dysinnervation disorders (CCDDs), a set of Mendelian disorders altering cMN development. We generated single cell epigenomic profiles for ∼86,000 cMNs and related cell types, identifying ∼250,000 accessible regulatory elements with cognate gene predictions for ∼145,000 putative enhancers. Seventy-five percent of elements (44 of 59) validated in an in vivo transgenic reporter assay, demonstrating that single cell accessibility is a strong predictor of enhancer activity. Applying our cMN atlas to 899 whole genome sequences from 270 genetically unsolved CCDD pedigrees, we achieved significant reduction in our variant search space and nominated candidate variants predicted to regulate known CCDD disease genes MAFB, PHOX2A, CHN1, and EBF3 – as well as new candidates in recurrently mutated enhancers through peak- and gene-centric allelic aggregation. This work provides novel non-coding variant discoveries of relevance to CCDDs and a generalizable framework for nominating non-coding variants of potentially high functional impact in other Mendelian disorders.

citations.title: In vivo mapping of mutagenesis sensitivity of human enhancers

narrative:
Short impact summary

This research presents a novel cell type-aware framework aimed at identifying non-coding variants implicated in Mendelian regulatory disorders. Published initially as a preprint on medRxiv, it has garnered citations, indicating its relevance to ongoing studies in the field.

Full narrative

The investigation into Mendelian regulatory disorders has taken a significant step forward with the introduction of a cell type-aware framework designed to nominate non-coding variants. The framework, detailed in a preprint released on medRxiv in 2023, reflects an innovative approach to understanding the underlying genetic factors associated with these disorders. The preprint has since progressed to formal publication in a well-regarded journal, enhancing its visibility and academic credibility.

The research has effectively attracted academic attention, as evidenced by 10 citations in specialized literature. Notably, one of the top citing venues is Nature, highlighting the work's significance within the academic community. This suggests that the framework is influencing further research and contributing to advancements in the field, surpassing merely anecdotal relevance.

The authorship includes notable institutions such as the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory and Harvard University, among others. This robust institutional backing signifies a collaborative effort that combines expertise from multiple prestigious centers, reinforcing the study's findings and methodological rigor.

Furthermore, the publication timeline has demonstrated its impact; the preprint underwent rigorous peer review, resulting in 9 citations for the final published version, reflecting a sustained interest in the study's conclusions. This trajectory not only underscores the framework's relevance but also exemplifies a commitment to disseminating research findings swiftly within the scientific community. Such rapid uptake is a hallmark of effective academic discourse and illustrates the significance of early sharing in advancing knowledge.
